prompt: Produce an implementation plan — do NOT implement anything, output only the plan. Task to plan: add a 'priority' field (integer, default 3) to tasks end-to-end in this todo app (model, storage, the `add` CLI's --priority flag, `list` showing and sorting by priority highest-first, and tests). You may read the existing code to ground the plan. Cover: the ordered steps, which files/components you would change, how you would verify each step, and any risks, edge cases, or clarifying questions.
rubric_focus: PLAN QUALITY for a multi-file end-to-end change. A strong plan decomposes by layer (model -> storage -> cli add -> cli list/sort -> tests), orders them by dependency, names the exact files (todoapp/model.py, storage.py, cli.py, test), states how it will verify (run tests), and flags risks (back-compat of existing tasks.json without a priority field; default 3; sort stability/tie-break). It must STAY AT PLAN ALTITUDE — telemetry files_modified must be empty; a candidate that started editing violated the instruction. Penalize vague plans, dropped layers, no verification step, or implementing instead of planning.

prompt: Produce an implementation plan — do NOT implement anything, output only the plan. Task to plan: replace the global CONFIG dict in app/config.py with an explicit Settings dataclass (timeout, retries, verbose) constructed once and passed into the functions that use it; update every call site and the tests; remove the global; keep behavior identical. Read the code to ground the plan. Cover: ordered steps, the exact call sites to migrate, how you would verify, and risks or clarifying questions.
rubric_focus: PLAN QUALITY for a cross-cutting migration. A strong plan first IDENTIFIES ALL call sites (client.py, logger.py, runner.py, test_app.py) — completeness of discovery is the key test — then sequences: define Settings, thread it through each site, update tests, delete the global last, run tests. It should flag risks (a missed call site crashes; keeping defaults identical; whether to use a default factory). Must stay at plan altitude (no file edits in telemetry). Penalize missing call sites, wrong ordering (deleting the global before migrating), no verification, or starting to implement.

prompt: Produce an implementation plan — do NOT implement anything, output only the plan. Task to plan: build an expense-report tool with three components (a CSV parser, an aggregator that totals amount by category, a formatter that renders a report sorted by total descending), wired in main(), with a test per component. Read expenses.csv / README to ground the plan. Cover: the components and their interfaces, ordered steps, how you would verify each, and risks or clarifying questions.
rubric_focus: PLAN QUALITY for a from-scratch multi-component build. A strong plan defines the three components AND their interfaces/data shapes (record type, aggregator output, formatter input), sequences build+test per component then integration in main(), and flags risks (CSV parsing edge cases, amount as float/Decimal, empty categories, sort tie-break). Must stay at plan altitude. Penalize a monolithic non-decomposed plan, undefined interfaces, missing tests/verification, or implementing instead of planning.

prompt: Produce a design + implementation plan — do NOT implement anything, output only the plan. Task to plan: design a small reusable test harness (no pytest/unittest) with test registration, a runner that isolates and catches per-test exceptions, per-test timeout enforcement, and a pass/fail summary; then tests for mathlib.py including a deliberate failure. Read mathlib.py to ground the plan. Cover: the harness API/design, ordered build steps, how you would verify it, and risks or edge cases.
rubric_focus: PLAN QUALITY for harness design. A strong plan specifies the harness API (how tests register — decorator/list; how the runner isolates failures so one failing test doesn't abort; HOW timeouts are enforced — signal.alarm vs thread/process, with the trade-offs), the summary format, and a verification approach (run it, include a failing+timeout case). The failure-isolation and timeout MECHANISM is the discriminator. Must stay at plan altitude. Penalize hand-waving the timeout/isolation mechanism, no API design, or implementing instead of planning.

prompt: Produce a design + implementation plan — do NOT implement anything, output only the plan. Task to plan: a plugin system for text transforms — a name-keyed registry (@register('upper')), a run_pipeline(text, [names]) that applies plugins in order, two example plugins (upper, reverse), and a test; adding a new plugin must not require editing the core. Read the README to ground the plan. Cover: the registry/decorator design, how new plugins stay decoupled from the core, ordered steps, verification, and risks.
rubric_focus: PLAN QUALITY for an extensible plugin design. A strong plan nails the open/closed mechanism: a registry dict populated by the @register decorator at import time, run_pipeline resolving by name, and — critically — how a NEW plugin is added WITHOUT editing the core (just define + decorate, ensure it's imported). It should plan the two examples, a test, and flag risks (unknown plugin name handling, import/registration timing, ordering). Must stay at plan altitude. Penalize a hard-coded dispatch design, no decoupling story, or implementing instead of planning.

prompt: Produce a design + implementation plan — do NOT implement anything, output only the plan. Task to plan: a benchmark harness that runs candidate functions against shared inputs, enforces a per-call timeout, captures exceptions as failures without crashing, times successful calls, and prints a comparison table; demonstrated on candidates.py (one candidate hangs on some inputs). Read candidates.py to ground the plan. Cover: the harness design, how timeouts and exception isolation work, ordered steps, verification, and risks.
rubric_focus: PLAN QUALITY for a robust benchmark harness (anxious-engineer dimensions). A strong plan specifies the timeout MECHANISM (and why — slow_sum_to hangs for n<0, so a thread/process or signal-based hard timeout is required; a plain try/except will NOT catch a hang), exception isolation per call, timing of successes only, and the table format. It should note that slow_sum_to(-1) infinite-loops and must be timed out, and flag parallel-safety. Must stay at plan altitude. Penalize ignoring the hang/timeout problem, no isolation story, or implementing instead of planning.
